summary: Graham argues that seeding startups is not enough to create a local Silicon Valley because early-stage companies are mobile; to keep them, a city must fund them at a scale that makes relocation unnecessary. He suggests a plausible shortcut: pay substantial sums to proven startups (chosen by top external angels) to move, creating a critical mass that can become self-sustaining. The real bottleneck is selecting good startups and having a city that is attractive enough to retain them.
key_points: Seed funding alone doesn't anchor startups; mobility makes local impact minimal.
To retain startups, cities must provide large enough funding to remove incentives to move.
The main constraint is selecting strong startups, which governments are ill-equipped to do.
Piggybacking on eminent angels' portfolios is a practical way to choose candidates.
City livability and culture determine how feasible and affordable the plan is.
money_quotes: The seed funding business is not a regional business, because at that stage startups are mobile.
If your terms force startups to do things they don't want to, only the desperate ones will take your money.
For the price of a football stadium, any town that was decent to live in could make itself one of the biggest startup hubs in the world.
Preposterous as this plan sounds, it's probably the most efficient way a city could select good startups.
Startup founders are mostly hackers, and hackers are much more constrained by gentlemen's agreements than regulations.
key_insight: You can’t cheaply manufacture a startup hub; the only plausible shortcut is to pay enough to relocate proven startups and create the density that makes the ecosystem self-sustaining.
